note_data.title: Chemical calculations
keyPoints: The **mole** is the unit for amount of substance, equal to 6.02 × 10²³ particles (atoms, molecules, or ions)
**Avogadro constant** = 6.02 × 10²³ mol⁻¹, representing the number of particles in one mole of any substance
**Molar mass** is the mass of one mole of a substance in g/mol, numerically equal to relative molecular mass
**Balanced chemical equations** show the mole ratio of reactants to products using whole number coefficients
**Concentration** is measured in mol/dm³ and calculated using moles of solute divided by volume of solution in dm³
**Percentage yield** = (actual yield ÷ theoretical yield) × 100%, always less than 100% due to practical losses
In calculations, always convert mass to moles first using the formula: moles = mass ÷ molar mass
**Limiting reagent** is the reactant that is completely used up first and determines the maximum product formed
For gas calculations at room temperature and pressure, one mole of any gas occupies 24 dm³

definitions.definition: The amount of substance that contains 6.02 × 10²³ particles
The mass of one mole of a substance measured in g/mol
The amount of solute dissolved in a given volume of solution, measured in mol/dm³
The reactant that is completely consumed first in a chemical reaction

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all working steps: moles = mass ÷ molar mass, use mole ratios, convert back to required units
Find the answer using calculations or logical reasoning, showing clear method
Give the numerical answer with correct units and significant figures

modelAnswer.question: Calculate the mass of calcium carbonate needed to produce 2.2 g of carbon dioxide when heated. CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ [Ar: C = 12, O = 16, Ca = 40]
modelAnswer.answer: Molar mass of CO₂ = 12 + (16 × 2) = 44 g/mol. Moles of CO₂ = 2.2 ÷ 44 = 0.05 mol. From equation, mole ratio CaCO₃ : CO₂ = 1:1, so moles of CaCO₃ = 0.05 mol. Molar mass of CaCO₃ = 40 + 12 + (16 × 3) = 100 g/mol. Mass of CaCO₃ = 0.05 × 100 = 5.0 g
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct moles of CO₂ = 0.05 mol

commonMistakes: Never confuse relative molecular mass with molar mass - molar mass has units g/mol
Students often forget to balance equations before using mole ratios in calculations
Always convert cm³ to dm³ by dividing by 1000 - never use cm³ directly in concentration calculations
Never round intermediate answers - only round the final answer to appropriate significant figures
Students often use atomic mass instead of molecular mass when calculating moles of compounds
examinerTips: Mole calculations appear in every Paper 2 - master the basic moles = mass ÷ molar mass formula
Always show working clearly - partial marks are given for correct method even if final answer is wrong
Check your answer makes sense - if calculating mass, answer should have mass units (g or kg)
Concentration calculations are common - practice converting between mol/dm³ and g/dm³
formulas: volume of gas = moles × 24 dm³ (at RTP)
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: No specific diagram required, but always show clear calculation layout with formula → substitution → answer format for full marks
